title: Tragacantha peregrina
taxonomy: Tragacantha peregrina is a synonym of Astragalus peregrinus, which is a species of flowering plant in the legume family (Fabaceae). The genus Astragalus is a large and diverse group of plants commonly known as milkvetches. Astragalus peregrinus is native to Europe and western Asia, but has been introduced to other regions as a non-native species. The plant is classified as a perennial herb with a sprawling growth habit. Astragalus peregrinus is also known by several other names, including Tragacantha peregrina and Astragalus vagus.
uses: Tragacantha peregrina, also known as Astragalus gummifer, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its roots are believed to have anti-inflammatory properties and are used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. In addition, it is thought to have antimicrobial and antifungal properties, making it useful in treating wounds and infections. The plant is also used to treat digestive issues and is believed to have a positive effect on the immune system. Further research is needed to confirm its efficacy and safety for human consumption.

properties: Tragacantha peregrina, also known as the stickle thistle, has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues. constituents: Tragacantha peregrina contains various active constituents including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids. These compounds are responsible for the plant's medicinal properties. parts: Tragacantha peregrina, also known as Astragalus gummifer, is a plant with potential medicinal properties. preparations: Tragacantha peregrina, also known as Prickly Pear Cactus or Barbary Thorn, has been used in traditional medicine for centuries. Tragacanth is a type of gum resin obtained from the plant Astragalus gummifer, a shrub native to the Middle East and parts of Asia. This plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its health benefits, including aiding digestion, reducing inflammation, and promoting wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Tragacanth include polysaccharides, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Tragacanth is commonly used in herbal preparations, such as syrups, lozenges, and ointments, particularly in traditional Arabic and Ayurvedic medicine.
